original_abstract:
In recent decades, Children’s Theology—alternatively known as child theology or theologising with children—has established itself as a cornerstone of religious education, particularly within the German academic landscape. This discipline shifts the educational focus toward the child’s fundamental right to religion, viewing the pedagogical process as a collaborative journey rather than a one-way transmission of doctrine. At its core, this approach seeks to move beyond a merely practiced spirituality to a reflected one, treating children as capable theological subjects who can independently construct meaning.

To truly engage with children’s spiritual development, educators must not only understand the unique "theology" children generate but also enter into active dialogue with their views. This article explores the internal differentiation of the field—categorizing it into theology *by*, *for*, and *with* children—and examines how these frameworks promote theological competence. By analyzing various didactic methods and the role of the teacher as both a partner and an expert, the paper outlines the challenges and opportunities inherent in fostering a "community of inquiry" where children’s questions serve as the primary catalyst for religious learning.
humanized_abstract:
Since the 1990s, Children’s Theology in the broader sense of child theology or theologising with children has developed into a major field in Religious Education in Germany and other countries. From the perspective of children’s inherent right to religion, the pedagogical relationship between teachers and pupils is redefined from a unilateral informative direction to a bilateral process in which both teacher and pupil have the same right to explore and learn, leading to a focus on the children’s spiritual lives and developing religious education into a discipline that fosters children’s reflection on their lived, practised religion and regards children as their own own theological thinkers capable of forming their own religious views in an atmosphere of mutual understanding between teacher and pupils.

To really educate children spiritually, one not only needs to understand the “theology” that children themselves make sense of their world, but to engage and dialogue with that children’s ‘theology’ or world view. This article explores the processes of differentiation within the field (children’s theology*by*, *for* and *with* God) and how these contribute to the attainment of theological literacy. An examination of educational approaches and the part played by teachers as both partner and as expert will help highlight the challenges and possibilities for creating a ‘community of inquiry’ where children’s questioning can become the main impetus for the learning of religion.
